coredata.dc:title: LED application and temperature-sensitive properties of white carbon dots doped with aluminum triacetylacetone without the N element
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Xiao
Yu, Hongquan
Cong, Shanshan
Liu, Tianshuo
Xu, Sai
Li, Xiangping
Chen, Baojiu
coredata.dc:description: CD phosphors are well light up in solution but tend to undergo powder-based solid-state aggregation quenching. Solid-state aggregation quenching is an important flaw in LED applications. Moreover, most one-component, white CD phosphors are obtained using doping of various valences of N. White CDs that are not doped with N have yet to be reported. In this work, we attempted to develop white aluminum triacetylacetone-doped CDs (Al-CDs) phosphors with resisted solid-state aggregation quenching for LED applications. The Al-CDs do not contain N element and emit broadband white light from 420 to 800 nm, and their emission centers can redshift or blueshift with increasing contents of aluminum triacetylacetone. Al-CDs phosphors exhibit excellent luminescent stability at high temperature (570 K) and under natural conditions (80 days). Their afterglow time increases as the concentration of aluminum triacetylacetone increases and can reach 10 seconds. In addition, the luminescent mechanism and temperature-sensitive properties of the Al-CDs phosphors were investigated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Al-CDs
No solid-state aggregation quenching
Broadband white light
Luminescent mechanism
WLED
Temperature sensitivity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation flow chart of the Al-CDs.
(a) XRD patterns of the Al-CDs with different aluminum acetylacetonate concentrations; (b) TEM image, (c) HR–TEM image, and (c) size distributions of the 0.2Al-CDs.

(a), (b) and (c) are the PL spectra, emission wavelengths and FWMHs, respectively, and CIE coordinates of the Al-CDs under 365 nm excitation. (d) Emission spectra of 0.2Al-CDs depend on temperature under 365 nm excitation; (e) Normalized integrated luminescence intensity ratios of Al-CDs depend on temperature under 365 nm excitation; (f) Emission spectra and (g) integral intensities of 0.2Al-CDs obtained at different calcination temperature (λex = 365 nm); (h) Emission spectra and (i) integral intensities of Al-CDs obtained from with different aluminum triacetylacetone concentrations; (j) CIE (x, y) coordinate diagram of the emission color CIE (x, y) of 0.2Al-CDs depend on temperature. The error bars in the figure were obtained by taking the standard deviations from multiple measurements.

(a) Full XPS spectra of the 0.05Al-CDs, 0.2Al-CDs, and 0.8Al-CDs. High-resolution B 1 s (b), Al 2p (c), C 1 s (d-f) and O 1 s (g-i) spectra of the 0.05Al-CDs, 0.4Al-CDs, and 0.8Al-CDs. (j) FTIR spectra of all the AlCDs. (k) EPR spectrum of the 0.2Al-CDs.
Schematic diagram of the formation mechanism and emission process of the Al-CDs (EX.: Excitation; Fluo.: Fluorescence; and Phos.: Phosphorescence).
(a) Diffuse reflectance spectra, (b) UVvis absorption spectra and (c, d) Tauc ([α(hv)]2–hv) curves of the Al-CDs.
Digital photographs of 0.2Al-CDs under sunlight and 365 nm UV light on and off.

Phosphorescence (a) and fluorescence (b) decay curves of Al-CDs doped with different aluminum triacetylacetone concentrations. (c) Phosphorescence and fluorescence lifetimes and their uncertainties for Al-CDs doped with different aluminum triacetylacetone concentrations. The error bars in the figure were obtained by taking the standard deviations from multiple measurements.

(a) Spectrum and photograph (inset) of WLED prepared using 0.2Al-CDs; (b) Corresponding PL emission color coordinates in the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram; (c) Integral emission intensities of the LED device at different input currents.

(a) Lifetime decay curves of 0.2Al-CD phosphors in the temperature range of 300–570 K; (b) Relationships curve of 1/τ (T) and temperature (K); (c) and (d) are absolute sensitivity (Sa) and relative sensitivity (Sr) curves of 0.2Al-CDs depending on temperature. (e) The uncertainties of the Sa and Sr curves of the 0.2Al-CDs depend on temperature.
Luminescent thermal stability of CDs doped with various elements.

head.ce:title: LED application and temperature-sensitive properties of white carbon dots doped with aluminum triacetylacetone without the N element

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian, Liaoning 116026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Dalian Maritime University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian 116026, Liaoning, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Astronomy, University College London, Gower St., London WC1E 6BT, United Kingdom

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Astronomy, University College London

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Astronomy, University College London, Gower St., London WC1E 6BT, United Kingdom

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Dalian Maritime University No. 1, Linghai Road, Dalian, Liaoning 116026, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CD phosphors are well light up in solution but tend to undergo powder-based solid-state aggregation quenching. Solid-state aggregation quenching is an important flaw in LED applications. Moreover, most one-component, white CD phosphors are obtained using doping of various valences of N. White CDs that are not doped with N have yet to be reported. In this work, we attempted to develop white aluminum triacetylacetone-doped CDs (Al-CDs) phosphors with resisted solid-state aggregation quenching for LED applications. The Al-CDs do not contain N element and emit broadband white light from 420 to 800 nm, and their emission centers can redshift or blueshift with increasing contents of aluminum triacetylacetone. Al-CDs phosphors exhibit excellent luminescent stability at high temperature (570 K) and under natural conditions (80 days). Their afterglow time increases as the concentration of aluminum triacetylacetone increases and can reach 10 seconds. In addition, the luminescent mechanism and temperature-sensitive properties of the Al-CDs phosphors were investigated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since carbon dots (CDs) were first described by Sun et al. in 2006 [1], they have received much attention in recent years because of their potential applications in various fields, such as electrocatalysis, photocatalysis, batteries, LEDs, sensing, photovoltaics, biomedicine and environmental monitoring, due to their abundant resources, low cost, large specific surface area, chemical resistance, and facile surface functionalization [2–7]. Metal elements such as Fe, Mg, Mn, Al, Ag, Ni, Zn and nonmetallic elements such as N, S, B, Si, Se, and P were doped into CDs to improve their surface performance and expand their application [8–19]. For instance, the doping of a variety of heteroatoms (B, S, N, etc.) provides enhanced active sites, which efficiently impart specific electrocatalytic or photocatalytic characteristics to CDs [20–25]. Fluorescent CDs with high luminescence can easily penetrate live cells, can be used as fluorescence imaging agents in vivo and are nontoxic to cells [26–29]. The doped CDs of metal atoms (Zn, Mg, Ag, Au, Cu, Ni, Ga, etc.) doped CDs have better electrocatalysis and photocatalytic activity than do those doped with commonly used heteroatoms because the metals have more electrons as donors and more unoccupied d orbitals, which facilitates the electron transfer process [10,30–34]. In addition, metal doping may enhance the photoluminescence quantum yield of CDs, which is attributed to the newly formed emission energy traps caused by structural changes and improved electron-hole purging of the CDs [35,36]. To date, various natural carbon sources, such as inorganic matter, organic molecules, and biomass etc were used as raw materials for preparing CDs through “top-down” or “bottom-up” approaches [37–39]. These studies indicate that the raw materials and synthesis routes significantly influence the structure and physical and chemical properties of CDs [26,28,34] and that these properties can be further improved by doping or surface passivation [35,39]. However, most of the abovementioned luminescent CDs are well lighted in solution but tend to undergo solid-state aggregation quenching in the powder state because of the stacking of π–π conjugate structures and excessive resonant energy transfer [40,41]. In addition, research related to the luminescence thermal stability of CDs has not yet been reported, which is very important for the application of CDs in LED devices.

In recent years, to fix the aforementioned problem of solid-state aggregation quenching of CDs, CDs have usually been transformed into solid grids by a physical method. The strategy is to increase the distance between CD particles and eliminate the self-suppressing effect that occurs when particles stick together [24,25]. The CDs were dispersed into inorganic salts, mesoporous materials, mixed organic–inorganic salts, and 2D layered materials [42]. There are several problems with this technique, namely, the need for the matrices and CDs to match, a rise in grain size, and the introduction of impurities after the CDs have been coated by the matrices, which is unfavorable for the application of CDs in LED devices [43].

Recently, a few reports of the use of multicolor-emitting CDs or single-component white CDs were reported for use in making WLEDs [43–46]. Compared to multicolor CDs, single-component white CDs have a high color rendering index (CRI > 90), low correlated color temperature (CCT <5000 K), and no color drift [47]. Furthermore, the single-component white CDs exhibit no phase separation and have better luminescent stability, which simplifies the fabrication procedure of WLEDs and reduces the cost of WLED devices.

In this work, we describe single-component white Al-CDs with ultrabroadband emission from 420 to 800 nm which is prepared by a straightforward, one-step pyrolysis method. The white Al-CDs exhibit better high temperature resistance characteristics (570 K) or long-term natural placement (80 days) than previously reported other CDs, such as CDs obtained from waste biomass [9,13,19], which are more suitable for LED applications. Notably, the Al-CDs had a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 239 nm under 365 nm excitation light. With changes in the aluminum triacetylacetone concentration, the positions of the white emission centers of the CDs take blue shift or red shift. In particular, the luminescence mechanism, temperature-sensitive properties and afterglow performance of the Al-CDs were investigated.

Citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.5 %), boric acid (H3BO3, 99.97 %), and aluminum triacetylacetone (C15H21AlO6, 99.99 %) were provided by Tianjin Chemical Reagent Factory, Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory and Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co, Ltd., respectively. The clear silicone (DOW CORN-ING OE-6550 A and B) for the LED package was supplied by Dow Corning Toray Co., Ltd. All of the aforementioned chemicals were used without any additional purification.

A one-step pyrolysis procedure was used to create Al-CDs utilizing citric acid, boric acid, and aluminum acetylacetonate, as shown in Fig. 1. In a typical synthesis procedure, 100 ml of deionized water was used to dissolve 6 g of boric acid, 2 g of citric acid, and a small amount of aluminum acetylacetonate. The mass ratios of aluminum acetylacetonate to water were selected to be 0.05:1, 0.1:1, 0.2:1, 0.4:1, 0.6:1, and 0.8:1. The above mixed solutions were stirred for 30 minutes and then pyrolyzed for 5 h at 230 °C. The as-prepared products were ground for 1 h to obtain the final white Al-CDs. In addition, to investigate the influence of the pyrolysis reaction temperature on the luminescence properties of the Al-CDs, 0.2Al-CDs were synthesized at 190 °C and 210 °C, respectively. The raw materials and experimental conditions for the 0.2Al-CDs prepared at 190 °C and 210 °C are the same as those for the 0.2Al-CDs obtained at 230 °C.

X-ray diffraction (XRD; CuK; λ= 0.15405 nm) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEOL JEM-2100 F) were used to study the crystal structures, morphology and size of the Al-CDs. The surface composition and elemental valence state of the Al-CDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Semroni Escalab 250 Xi). Diffuse reflectance spectroscopy of Al-CDs was performed using a Shimadzu UV-3600 UVVis-NIR spectrophotometer to calculate the optical bandgap, with BaSO4 powder serving as the standard reference. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) measurements of the Al-CDs were performed using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20 infrared spectrometer. The photoluminescence spectra of the Al-CDs were recorded in an Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer with a 450 W xenon lamp as the light source. The fluorescence lifetimes of the Al-CDs were determined using a calendar F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer with a 150 W xenon lamp as the light source. The uncertainties in the photoluminescence intensities, phosphorescence and fluorescence lifetimes of the Al-CDs were obtained by taking standard deviations from multiple measurements.

The XRD pattern of the Al-doped CDs is illustrated in Fig. 2a. The broad peak at approximately 22.6° were assigned to the graphene layers with short-range stacking (JCPDS No. 50–00926) [48]. The diffraction peak located at 13.3° is indexed to the (002) crystal face of graphene oxide [49–51]. The diffraction peak at approximately 27.5° was attributed to the in-plane properties of graphene [51]. These results agreed with previously reported boron- and nitrogen-co-doped graphene quantum dots [26,51]. As shown in Fig. 2b-d, the average diameter of the Al-CDs is approximately 2.04 nm. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is indexed to the carbon crystalline plane (100) (Fig. 2c) [52].

The PL spectra of Al-CDs with various aluminum acetylacetonate concentrations are shown in Fig. 3a. These CD particles showed broad emission bands spanning from 420 nm to 800 nm after being excited by 365 nm UV light. These emission peak centers are changed to the redshift from 550 to 580 nm as the aluminum acetylacetonate concentration increases from 0.05 to 0.2, while the emission peak centers underwent a blueshift from 580 nm to 565 nm as the aluminum acetylacetonate concentration increases from 0.2 to 0.8. The FWHM values of the 0.05Al-CDs, 0.1Al-CDs, 0.2Al-CDs, 0.4Al-CDs, 0.6Al-CDs, and 0.8Al-CDs are 193, 194, 239, 246, 222, and 227 nm, respectively (Fig. 3b). The FWHM of the Al-CDs can be adjusted by changing the aluminum acetylacetonate concentration. The uncertainties in the FWHM of the Al-CDs are shown in Table S1. The FWHM of the Al-CDs was broader than that of the CDs doped with other metals, such as Mn, Ag, Fe, Zn, Cu, La, Eu, and Tb (Table S1). The CIE values of the Al-CDs were (0.37, 0.44) for 0.05Al-CDs, (0.37, 0.45) for 0.1Al-CDs, (0.41, 0.44) for 0.2Al-CDs, (0.40, 0.44) for 0.4Al-CDs, (0.39, 0.44) for 0.6Al-CDs, and (0.38, 0.43) for 0.8Al-CDs, as shown in Fig. 3c. The CCT can be controlled between 3789 and 4608 K by adjusting the aluminum acetylacetonate concentration (Table S2). The CRI value was also controlled between 77.65 and 86.43 (Table S2). The dependence of the emission spectra of the Al-CDs on temperature is shown in Fig. 3d and Fig. S2. With increasing temperature, the emission peak shapes of all the Al-CD powders were almost unaltered, and the emission intensity steadily decreased. All Al-CDs can maintain more than 50 % of their initial emission intensity even at 450 K (Fig. 3e and Fig. S2), indicating their excellent thermal stability. The luminescent thermal stability of the Al-CDs was continuously improved by increasing the aluminum acetylacetonate concentration (Fig. 3e and Table S3). The luminescent thermal stability of Al-CDs outperforms that of CDs without aluminum acetylacetonate and CDs doped with other metal ions ( Table 1), which has important advantages for LED applications. Furthermore, the emission centers of all the Al-CDs did not shift under different excitation wavelengths, indicating that the emission center location is dictated by the Al-O vibration (Fig. S3). The influence of the pyrolysis reaction temperature on the luminescence properties of the Al-CDs was also investigated. As the pyrolyzation temperature increases from 190 °C to 230 °C, the emission centers of the obtained 0.2Al-CDs redshift from 510 nm to 580 nm (Fig. 3 f). The photoluminescence intensity of the 0.2Al-CDs first increased and then decreased (Fig. 3 g and Table S4) as the reaction temperature increased. Moreover, the emission intensity of the Al-CDs increases as the doped concentration of aluminum acetylacetonate increases from 0.05 wt% to 0.8 wt% (Fig. 3 h and 3i, and Table S5). In addition, the Al-CDs exhibit excellent emission color stability at high temperature. The emission color of the 0.2Al-CDs almost does not experience a chromaticity shift at 570 K (Fig. 3j). Under natural conditions, the emission intensities of all the Al-CDs did not decrease significantly within 20 days and remained at 62 %-68 % of their initial values even after 80 days, indicating that the Al-CDs have outstanding luminous stability (Fig. S4). The photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of the 0.05Al-CDs, 0.1Al-CDs, 0.5Al-CDs, 0.6Al-CDs, 0.7Al-CDs and 0.9Al-CDs were 3.25 %, 3.47 %, 3.65 %, 6.31 %, 6.73 % and 9.02 %, respectively.

To analyze the shift behavior of the emission centers of the Al-CDs, the XPS spectra of the 0.05Al-CDs, 0.2Al-CDs, and 0.8Al-CDs were investigated, as shown in Fig. 4a-i. Al, O, C, and B signals were detected in the full XPS spectra of the Al-CDs (Fig. 4a). The atomic contents of B and Al are 26.76 % and 1.28 %, respectively, for the 0.05 Al-CDs, 28.44 % and 2.34 %, respectively, for the 0.2 Al-CDs, and 25.56 % and 3.45 %, respectively, for the 0.8 Al-CDs. From Fig. 4b and c, the B 1 s signals at 193.1 eV and the Al 2p signals at 75.2 eV are assigned to B–O and Al–O bonds, respectively [59,60]. From Fig. 4d–f, the peaks at 284.5, 285.7, and 286 eV in the C 1 s signals can be indexed to the CC/C–C, C–O, and CO bonds, respectively [61]. Yuan et al. reported that a change in the relative content of CO bonds can lead to a blueshift and redshift of the emission peaks [62]. Here, the same phenomena are also found: the relative content of CO bonds first increased and then decreased with increasing aluminum acetylacetonate concentration, and the peak positions of the corresponding emission centers of the Al-CDs first red shifted and then blue shifted. When the ratio of Al to CDs is 0.2, the relative content of CO is the highest, and the corresponding Al-CDs have the largest emission wavelength. The above results indicate that the shifts of peak positions of the emission centers of the Al-CDs are related to the CO content (Table S6). The peaks at 530.5, 531.8, 532.6, and 533.8 eV in the O 1 s high-resolution spectra are well indexed to Al–O, CO, B–O, and C–O bonds, respectively (Fig. 4g–i) [59–61]. FT-IR spectra were used to further characterize the chemical state of the Al-CD surfaces, as shown in Fig. 4j. The shapes of all the Al-CDs are almost the same. The absorption peaks at 3223 cm−1, 2260 cm−1, 1463 cm−1, 1198 cm−1, and 463 cm−1 were assigned to the –OH, CO, –CH2, B-O, and Al–O groups, respectively, and agree with the XPS results [63–68]. The surface conditions of the Al-CDs were further characterized through EPR spectrum, as shown in Fig. 4k. A signal at g = 2.00382 is observed, which is believed to arise from the paramagnetic state of single-electron-occupied aluminum vacancies [69], indicating the existence of aluminum vacancies and the absence of free Al atoms in the Al-CDs. The formation of new Al-O bonds can cause a change of CO content of CDs, resulting in a redshift or blueshift of the emission peak of the CDs. Based on these results, the formation mechanism of the Al-CDs was suggested and is illustrated in Fig. 5. Citric acid, aluminum acetylacetonate and boric acid were mixed, polymerized and carbonized. During the polymerization and carbonization, acetylacetone and Al atom steam were incorporated into the carbon structure through oxygen groups by coupling reactions. Boron atom with vacant p orbit can attract π electrons to form a p-π conjugate system when CDs are dispersed in Boric Acid, which can effectively reduce the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level of the system and promote the inter system crossing (ISC) between S1 and T1. Acetylacetone contains conjugated π bonds with electron-donating chemical groups and can enlarge the π-conjugated sp2-carbon network, leading to a redshift of the emission centers. Conversely, Al-O bonds are electron-withdrawing oxygenated functional groups, which can result in a blueshift of the emission centers. The formation of new chemical bonds greatly changes the electron density distribution, energy gap and optical properties of CDs [70–72]. The CDs are enclosed in situ in B-O, CO and Al-O bonds and -OH groups, which leads to efficient ISC and reverse inter system crossing (RISC). The electrons in the S1 state dropped to the T1 state because of the ISC generated from the small energy gap between the singlet and triplet states. Then, some electrons in the T1 state are returned to the S1 state through the RISC to promote fluorescence emission. ISC and RISC effectively inhibit the nonradiative transitions and aggregation-induced quenching of Al-CDs in the solid state, resulting in efficient and ultrawide white-wavelength emission.

Fig. 6a shows the diffuse reflectance spectra of the Al-doped CDs, indicating that they can absorb light at wavelengths ranging from 200 to 450 nm. The corresponding UV–Vis spectra are shown in Fig. 6b. The absorption peak at 240 nm originated from the π–π* transitions of the CO and CC bonds of the CDs [73]. The absorption peak at 350 nm was related to the n→ π* electronic changes of the CDs [74,75].

The band gap energies of the Al-CDs were determined with the Tauc method, as shown in Figs. 6c and 6d. Here, α is the light absorption coefficient, and Eopt is the band gap energy. The relationship between α(hν) and (Eopt) is written as α(hν) = B(hν-Eopt) 1/2 [76]. where h is the Planck constant, ν is the frequency of the photon, and B is a constant. The Eopt values were calculated to be 1.54 eV for 0.05Al-CDs, 2.05 eV for 0.1Al-CDs, 2.28 eV for 0.2Al-CDs, 1.77 eV for 0.4Al-CDs, 1.81 eV for 0.6Al-CDs, and 1.93 eV for 0.8Al-CDs. This result agrees with the above phenomenon in which the Al-doped CD emission centers first redshift and then blueshift.

The afterglow behavior of the Al-CDs was also observed, as shown in Fig. 7 and Fig. S5. Under 365 nm UV light irradiation, the CDs doped with acetylacetone showed no afterglow behavior. CDs doped with aluminum hydroxide and CDs doped with aluminum triacetylacetone exhibit apparent afterglow performance. They can emit either cool-white or warm-white light. The afterglow time increases with increasing Al concentration and can reach 10 s, which indicates that the Al-CD solid powder can be used as a powerful anti-counterfeiting substance.

To reveal the luminescent mechanism of the Al-CDs, their phosphorescence and fluorescence decay curves were measured, as shown in Fig. 8. The average phosphorescence lifetimes (τavg) of the Al-CDs were fitted by a double exponential function and can be written as [77]:
(1)
τ
a v g
=
A
1
τ
1
2
+
A
2
τ
2
2
A
1
τ
1
+
A
2
τ
2
where τ1 and τ2 are the components of the decay times. A1 and A2 are the corresponding fitting parameters. The average fluorescence lifetime (τavg*) of the Al-CDs can be calculated by the following equation[78]:
(2)
τ
avg
=
∫
t
I
( t )
d t
∫
I
( t )
dt
where I(t) is the emission intensity at time t. The uncertainties are obtained by taking standard deviations from multiple measurements (Fig. S6, Fig. S7, and Table S7).

The average phosphorescence lifetimes of the 0.05Al-CDs, 0.1Al-CDs, 0.2Al-CDs, 0.4Al-CDs, 0.6Al-CDs and 0.8Al-CDs were calculated to be 0.49 ±0.10 s (λem= 550 nm), 1.61 ±0.08 s (λem= 560 nm), 1.90 ±0.13 s (λem= 580 nm), 1.56 ±0.11 s (λem= 575 nm), 1.73 ±0.12 s (λem= 570 nm), and 1.66 ±0.15 s (λem= 565 nm), respectively, as shown in Fig. 8a, Fig. S6 and Table S7. The afterglow of the Al-CDs was presumably induced by the Al dopant and was related to efficient intersystem crossing (ISC) and reverse intersystem crossing (RISC). As Fig. 5 shows, the electrons in the S1 state drop to the T1 state because of the ISC generated from the small energy gap between the singlet and triplet states [79,80] to effectively inhibit the nonradiative transitions, resulting in phosphorescence emission. The average fluorescence lifetimes of the 0.05 Al-CDs, 0.1Al-CDs, 0.2Al-CDs, 0.4Al-CDs, 0.6Al-CDs and 0.8Al-CDs were 11.0 ±0.9 ns (λem= 550 nm), 11.8 ±1.1 ns (λem= 560 nm), 12.2 ±1.1 ns (λem= 580 nm), 14.8 ±1.1 ns (λem= 575 nm), 15.8 ±0.8 ns (λem= 570 nm), and 17.7 ±1.6 ns (λem= 565 nm), respectively, as shown in Fig. 8b and Fig. S7. As the Al doping concentration increased, ISC and RISC were more effectively promoted, resulting in a significant increase in the fluorescence lifetime of the Al-CDs (Fig. 8b). Accordingly, the emission intensities of the Al-CDs increase with increasing aluminum concentration (Fig. 3i).

Furthermore, Al-CDs hold potential for use in the color conversion layers of white LED devices, as shown in Fig. 9a. The solid-state Al-CDs were dispersed into silicone and coated onto the surface of a 365 nm UV LED chip to create a warm WLED. A broad PL emission spectrum from 400 to 800 nm, which contains fluorescence and phosphorescence emissions, was obtained. The CIE coordinates, CCT and CRI values of the as-prepared WLED are (0.39, 0.42), and 3789.35 K, respectively, and the CRI is 86.4 (Fig. 9b). As the input current increases from 500 mA to 700 mA, the brightness of the WLED gradually increases (Fig. 9c).

Moreover, the temperature sensitivity of the 0.2Al-CDs was also investigated. The absolute sensitivity (Sa) and relative sensitivity (Sr) are the pivotal parameters for assessing the practical performance of fluorescence thermometers and are defined as follows [81]:
(3)
S
a
=
d τ
dT
(4)
S
r
=
d τ
τ dT

There is no mature physical model for describing CD thermometers. Here, a 3rd-order polynomial function was used to fit the 1/τ values:
(5)
1
τ
=
B
0
+
B
1
* T +
B
2
*
T
2
+
B
3
*
T
3
B0, B1, B2 and B3 represent the fitting coefficients, and T is the Kelvin temperature.

In addition, the uncertainties in the Sa and Sr curves of the 0.2Al-CDs depend on temperature, i.e., the temperature uncertainty (δT) was also calculated, which is defined as follows[82]:
(6)
δ T =
1
S
r
δ τ
τ
where δτ/τ is the relative error depending on the testing instrument (0.5 %.).

Fig. 10a shows the lifetime curves (λem= 580 nm) of the 0.2Al-CDs. The τavg values decrease with increasing temperature from 300 to 570 K (Fig. S8 and Table S8). The relationship between 1/τ and T was well fitted by Eq. (5), as displayed in Fig. 10b. As a result, the maximum values of Sa and Sr are 1.79×10−3 K−1 and 8.06×10−3 K−1, respectively (Fig. 10c and d). The δT values for the 0.2Al-CD phosphors were calculated to be ± 0.621∼±5.22 K (Fig. 10e).

In summary, Al-CDs with ultrawide white light (420–800 nm) without aggregation-induced quenching in the solid-state form were prepared by a one-step pyrolysis technique. These CDs do not contain N element and differ from previously reported single-component white light CDs. The emission peak centers of the Al-CDs are red shifted from 550 nm to 580 nm and then blue shifted from 580 nm to 565 nm with increasing concentrations of aluminum triacetylacetone. The shift of peak position of the emission centers of the Al-CDs is affected by the CO bond content. The band gap energies of the Al-CDs were influenced by the aluminum triacetylacetone concentration. These Al-CDs exhibit excellent luminescence stability at high temperature (570 K) and long-term natural placement (80 days). In addition, the temperature sensitivity of the 0.2Al-CDs was also investigated. The maximum Sa and Sr of the Al-CDs can reach to 1.79×10−3 K−1 and 8.06×10−3 K−1, respectively. The 0.2Al-CD powder was combined with a 365 nm chip to prepare a WLED device. This WLED device exhibits CIE coordinates of (0.39, 0.41), a CCT of 3789.34 K, and a CRI of 86.43.

Xiao Gao: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Shanshan Cong: Formal analysis, Data curation. Hongquan Yu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Sai Xu: Writing – review & editing. Tianshuo Liu: Writing – review & editing. Baojiu Chen: Writing – review & editing. Xiangping Li: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 52071048, 11704056, 12274049 and 11774042) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (grant No. 3132023519).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2024.175405.
